questions.q: What is the greedy choice property?
options: An algorithm that runs in linear time
Making locally optimal choices at each step, hoping to find a global optimum
Choosing the largest element in the input
Using random selection to approximate a solution
questions.explanation: The greedy choice property means that a globally optimal solution can be constructed by making locally optimal (greedy) choices without reconsidering earlier decisions. Not all problems have this property — coin change with arbitrary denominations is a classic counterexample where greedy fails.

questions.q: What is Huffman coding and why does it use a greedy approach?
options: An encoding that ensures all codes have equal length; it sorts characters alphabetically
A variable-length prefix-free code that minimizes average code length; greedy selection builds a binary tree by repeatedly combining the two lowest-frequency nodes
A compression algorithm that removes all vowels from text
A cipher that shifts characters by a fixed amount
questions.explanation: Huffman coding creates optimal variable-length codes by greedily merging the two lowest-frequency characters into a subtree. Repeating this builds a tree where frequent characters get shorter codes. The greedy choice (merging lowest frequencies) is optimal because it minimizes the weighted path length. This is proven by an exchange argument.

questions.q: In the activity selection problem, what greedy choice leads to the optimal solution?
options: Select the activity with the earliest start time
Select the activity with the latest start time
Select the activity with the earliest end time; this leaves the most room for subsequent activities
Select the activity with the longest duration
questions.explanation: Activity selection aims to schedule the maximum number of non-overlapping activities. Sorting by end time and greedily selecting the activity finishing earliest is optimal — it removes the least time from the remaining interval, maximizing opportunities for future selections. This achieves O(n log n) time and is provably optimal.

questions.q: When does the greedy algorithm fail for the coin change problem?
options: When coin denominations include 1 (which always works)
On coin sets like {1, 3, 4} with amount 6 — greedy gives 4+1+1 (3 coins) but optimal is 3+3 (2 coins)
Greedy always finds the optimal solution for coin change
When the amount is greater than the sum of all coins
questions.explanation: Greedy coin change (always pick the largest coin that fits) is not optimal for arbitrary denominations. The counterexample {1, 3, 4} and amount 6 shows greedy fails. However, greedy is optimal for canonical coin systems (like US coins: 1, 5, 10, 25). For general denominations, dynamic programming is required.

questions.q: What makes the fractional knapsack problem solvable by a greedy algorithm while 0/1 knapsack is not?
options: Fractional knapsack has smaller input size
In fractional knapsack, you can split items and always fill capacity with the highest value-to-weight ratio items; 0/1 knapsack has discrete items where greedy may exclude better combinations
0/1 knapsack is always harder computationally
Fractional and 0/1 knapsack are the same problem
questions.explanation: Fractional knapsack allows splitting items, so greedily picking by value-to-weight ratio is optimal — at capacity W, just fill with the best-ratio items (fractionally if needed). 0/1 knapsack forbids splitting. A counterexample: items (10 kg, $100) and two items (5 kg, $40) each. Greedy on ratio picks the first item and half of one other; optimal might be both 5 kg items. DP is required for 0/1.

questions.q: What is a matroid, and why do greedy algorithms work on matroids?
options: A mathematical object with a specific exchange property; greedy maximization on matroids is provably optimal
A type of graph with many cycles
Any problem solvable in polynomial time
A scheduling problem with precedence constraints
questions.explanation: A matroid is a combinatorial structure with an exchange property: if set A is independent and |A| < |B|, there exists an element in B-A that can be added to A while remaining independent. Greedy algorithms — repeatedly adding the maximum-weight independent element — are optimal for matroids. MST and activity selection are matroid problems; 0/1 knapsack is not.

questions.q: In interval scheduling for room allocation, why is earliest-deadline-first greedy?
options: It sorts tasks by start time and processes them in order
It assigns tasks with earliest deadlines first, minimizing late completions — the greedy choice is to prevent a deadline miss
It assigns the longest tasks first
It randomly picks tasks to assign
questions.explanation: Earliest-deadline-first (EDF) is a greedy scheduling rule that prioritizes tasks with the soonest deadlines. For minimizing missed deadlines, this is optimal — deferring a closer deadline risks missing it, so addressing closer deadlines first greedy maximizes on-time tasks. EDF is optimal for single-machine scheduling without preemption.

questions.q: What is the difference between a greedy algorithm that is provably optimal and one that is only approximately optimal?
options: Provably optimal algorithms are faster; approximate algorithms are slower
Provably optimal finds the true best solution; approximate may not — proven via exchange argument or matroid theory versus guaranteed approximation ratio (e.g., 2-approximation)
Approximate algorithms use randomness; optimal ones do not
There is no difference; all greedy algorithms are approximate
questions.explanation: Some greedy algorithms provably find the global optimum (Huffman, activity selection, MST). Others guarantee only an approximation ratio: e.g., greedy set cover achieves ln(n)-approximation. Proving optimality requires an exchange argument showing that swapping any choice for another worsens the solution. Approximation ratios are proven via comparison to a lower bound on the optimal solution.

questions.q: Why does the greedy approach work for the MST problem?
options: MST always has the fewest edges

If you include any edge not in an MST, you create a cycle; if that edge is the minimum in the cycle, you can swap it for an MST edge without increasing weight — greedy selection of minimum edges is safe
MST is a tree so it has no cycles by definition
Greedy always works for any graph problem
questions.explanation: MST satisfies the greedy choice property via the cut property: for any partition of vertices, the minimum-weight edge crossing the partition is in some MST. Both Kruskal and Prim exploit this: they repeatedly select a minimum edge that is safe to include (Kruskal: does not form a cycle; Prim: connects the growing tree to new vertices). This greedy strategy is provably optimal.

questions.q: What is a counterexample showing that greedy does not work for traveling salesman (TSP)?
options: A triangle with equal edge weights
A directed graph with cycles

A path graph with 4 vertices where greedy nearest-neighbor picks the wrong starting edge and explores suboptimally, while a different tour is shorter — TSP is NP-hard, so no greedy solution is guaranteed optimal in polynomial time
Any complete graph
questions.explanation: TSP asks for the shortest Hamiltonian cycle. Greedy nearest-neighbor (always visit the closest unvisited city) fails on many inputs: consider 4 cities in a cross pattern. Starting from one corner, nearest-neighbor may construct a long tour while a diamond pattern is shorter. TSP is NP-hard, so no known polynomial-time algorithm solves it optimally. Greedy is only a heuristic with no approximation guarantee.
